title: Entry of merchandise — reasonable care
text: The importer of record must use reasonable care to: file entry within 15 calendar days after vessel arrival; declare value, classification, rate of duty; produce records on demand. Reasonable care includes consulting customs expertise, CBP rulings, court cases, brokers. Failure to exercise reasonable care = 19 USC §1592 penalty exposure (negligent, gross negligent, or fraudulent).

title: Entry process — ENTRY/IMMEDIATE DELIVERY/RELEASE
text: Entry types: Type 01 Consumption, Type 02 Quota, Type 03 ATB, Type 11 Informal. CBP Form 3461 (CBPF7501 entry summary). Documentation: commercial invoice, packing list, bill of lading, certificates of origin (FTA claims), license/permit (PGA), CTPAT info. 15-day deadline from arrival OR 10 days for entry summary after release.

title: Importer Security Filing (ISF) — 10+2
text: For ocean cargo destined for US: importer must submit ISF (10 data elements) 24 hours BEFORE vessel loading at foreign port; carrier submits 2 data elements (vessel stow plan + container status messages). Late/inaccurate ISF = liquidated damages up to $5K per shipment + cargo hold. Effective enforcement since 2010.

title: Classification + appraisement
text: HTSUS (Harmonized Tariff Schedule of the United States) classification governs duty rate. General Rules of Interpretation (GRI 1-6). Section + Chapter Notes. CROSS rulings database. Appraisement methods in 19 USC §1401a — Transaction Value (preferred), Transaction Value of Identical/Similar Merchandise, Deductive Value, Computed Value, Fallback. Related-party transactions get extra scrutiny.

title: Protest procedure
text: Importer may protest CBP decisions (classification, value, rate, refusal to reliquidate) within 180 days of liquidation. CBP Form 19. Protest must be in writing + specify decision + grounds. CBP decision on protest → Court of International Trade (180 days appeal). Protest does not stay collection — file w/ duty paid or surety bond.

title: Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act
text: Rebuttable presumption: any goods made wholly or in part in Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region OR by entities on UFLPA Entity List = forced labor. Banned from US import unless importer overcomes presumption by 'clear + convincing' evidence. Targeted sectors: cotton, polysilicon, tomatoes. Importers must prepare documentation BEFORE shipping.

title: Convict labor / forced labor / indentured labor — Section 307
text: Prohibition on importation of merchandise mined, produced, or manufactured (wholly or in part) by convict labor, forced labor, or indentured labor under penal sanctions. CBP issues Withhold Release Orders (WROs) on specific producers/regions. Importer must petition for release w/ evidence of non-forced-labor production.

title: USMCA preference qualification
text: Goods qualify for USMCA preference under one of: wholly obtained (Article 4.3); produced exclusively from originating materials (4.4); produced using non-originating materials that satisfy product-specific rules (4.5) — tariff shift OR regional value content. Automotive — 75% RVC + 70% steel/aluminum North-American. Apparel — yarn-forward. Importer Certification of Origin OR Producer/Exporter. Reasonable Care to verify.

title: Antidumping + countervailing duties
text: AD duties offset dumping (sales below normal value). CVD duties offset countervailable subsidies. Importer pays AD/CVD cash deposit at time of entry + faces retrospective true-up at administrative review (annual). Evasion = §1592 + EAPA (Enforce + Protect Act) allegations. Transshipment + minor processing in third countries = circumvention = covered.

title: WCO SAFE Framework of Standards
text: WCO SAFE: pillars 1) Customs-to-Customs network (advance electronic info, common risk targeting, mutual recognition); 2) Customs-to-Business partnerships (AEO — Authorized Economic Operator, equivalent to CTPAT). Standards: integrated supply chain mgmt, authority to inspect cargo, modern technology, risk mgmt, targeting, training. AEO mutual recognition agreements expedite processing.
